questions.question: What is Ensemble Learning in machine learning?
options: Combining multiple models to improve performance
questions.explanation: Ensemble learning combines predictions from multiple models to produce better results than any individual model.

questions.question: What is the core principle behind why ensembles often outperform single models?
options: The wisdom of crowds — diverse models cancel out each other's errors
questions.explanation: Different models make different errors. When aggregated, these errors tend to cancel out, improving overall accuracy.

questions.question: What is a Voting Classifier in ensemble learning?
options: An ensemble that aggregates predictions from multiple classifiers and predicts the class with the most votes
A classifier that selects the best model
A classifier that weights models by their training time
A classifier that uses only neural networks
questions.explanation: A Voting Classifier trains multiple diverse models and predicts the class that receives the most votes from the individual classifiers.

questions.question: What is the difference between Hard Voting and Soft Voting?
options: Hard voting uses predicted class labels; soft voting uses predicted probabilities averaged across classifiers
Hard voting uses probabilities; soft voting uses class labels
Hard voting is for regression only
questions.explanation: Hard voting counts class label votes. Soft voting averages predicted probabilities and selects the class with highest average probability, often performing better.

questions.question: When does Soft Voting outperform Hard Voting?
options: When classifiers are well-calibrated and can output probabilities
When there are only two classifiers
questions.explanation: Soft voting leverages confidence levels from probability estimates. Well-calibrated classifiers provide reliable probability scores that improve ensemble decisions.

questions.question: What is Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)?
options: Training multiple models on different random subsets of the training data with replacement, then aggregating predictions
questions.explanation: Bagging trains multiple instances of the same model on bootstrap samples (random sampling with replacement) and averages their predictions.

questions.question: What is a bootstrap sample?
options: A random sample drawn with replacement from the original dataset
A sample of only the most important features
questions.explanation: Bootstrap sampling randomly selects instances from the training set with replacement. Some instances may appear multiple times, others not at all.

questions.question: What percentage of training instances are typically left out of a bootstrap sample?
questions.explanation: With bootstrap sampling (sampling with replacement), approximately 63.2% of unique instances are selected, leaving about 36.8% (often rounded to 37%) out-of-bag.

questions.question: What is Out-of-Bag (OOB) evaluation in Bagging?
options: Using the instances not selected in a bootstrap sample to evaluate the corresponding model
Evaluating on a separate test set
questions.explanation: Each model in a bagging ensemble can be evaluated on the ~37% of instances not included in its bootstrap sample. This provides a built-in validation set.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using OOB evaluation?
options: It provides a free validation set without needing a separate train-test split
It requires an extra test set
questions.explanation: OOB evaluation leverages the out-of-bag instances for each model, eliminating the need for a separate validation set while providing an unbiased performance estimate.

questions.question: What is a Random Forest?
options: An ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets at each split
A neural network with many layers
questions.explanation: Random Forest is a bagging ensemble of Decision Trees. Each tree is trained on a bootstrap sample, and at each split, only a random subset of features is considered.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Bagging with Decision Trees and Random Forest?
options: Random Forest trains on the full dataset
Random Forest introduces additional randomness by using only a subset of features at each split
questions.explanation: While both use bagging, Random Forest adds feature randomness by considering only a random subset of features at each split, decorrelating the trees further.

questions.question: Why does Random Forest use random feature subsets at each split?
options: To decorrelate trees — without it, dominant features would be chosen by most trees
questions.explanation: If all features are considered, strong features would be selected by most trees, making them correlated. Random feature selection ensures diversity among trees.

questions.question: How does a Random Forest make predictions?
options: Using the tree with highest accuracy
Aggregating predictions from all trees — majority vote for classification, average for regression
Randomly selecting one tree per prediction
questions.explanation: Random Forest aggregates predictions from all trees. For classification, it uses majority voting. For regression, it averages predictions.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class implements Random Forest Classification?
questions.explanation: RandomForestClassifier in Scikit-Learn implements the Random Forest algorithm for classification tasks.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class implements Random Forest Regression?
questions.explanation: RandomForestRegressor implements Random Forest for regression, averaging predictions from all trees.

questions.question: What does the n_estimators parameter control in RandomForestClassifier?
options: The maximum depth of each tree
The number of features to consider
The number of trees in the forest
questions.explanation: n_estimators sets the number of trees in the forest. More trees generally improve performance but increase training time.

questions.question: What does the max_features parameter control in Random Forest?
options: The total number of features in the dataset
The number of features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
questions.explanation: max_features controls how many features are randomly selected for consideration at each split. Common values are 'sqrt' for classification and 'log2' or n for regression.

questions.question: What is the default max_features value for RandomForestClassifier in Scikit-Learn?
questions.explanation: For classification, the default max_features='sqrt' means only the square root of the total features are considered at each split.

questions.question: What is the default max_features value for RandomForestRegressor in Scikit-Learn?
questions.explanation: For regression, the default max_features=1.0 means all features are considered at each split.

questions.question: What is Extra-Trees (Extremely Randomized Trees)?
options: An ensemble where trees use random thresholds for splits rather than optimal ones
questions.explanation: Extra-Trees add more randomness by using random thresholds for each feature instead of searching for the best possible threshold, trading more bias for lower variance.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn class implements Extra-Trees for classification?
questions.explanation: ExtraTreesClassifier implements Extremely Randomized Trees for classification in Scikit-Learn.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Extra-Trees over Random Forest?
options: Faster training because thresholds are randomly chosen, not optimized
questions.explanation: Extra-Trees are faster to train because they don't search for optimal thresholds — they randomly select them and pick the best random threshold.

questions.question: What is Feature Importance in Random Forest?
options: The depth at which a feature is used
A measure of how much a feature decreases impurity across all trees
The number of trees that use a feature
questions.explanation: Feature importance measures how much each feature contributes to decreasing impurity (Gini/MSE) weighted by the number of samples reaching that node, averaged across all trees.

questions.question: How can you access feature importances in Scikit-Learn's RandomForestClassifier?
questions.explanation: The feature_importances_ attribute returns an array where each value represents the importance of the corresponding feature.

questions.question: What is Boosting in ensemble learning?
options: Training all models simultaneously on the same data
Using only the best single model
Sequentially training models, with each new model focusing on correcting errors of the previous ones
questions.explanation: Boosting trains models sequentially. Each new model gives more weight to instances that previous models misclassified, gradually reducing overall error.

questions.question: What is AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)?
options: A boosting algorithm that trains models sequentially, increasing weights of misclassified instances
questions.explanation: AdaBoost trains models sequentially. After each round, it increases weights of misclassified instances so subsequent models focus more on hard cases.

questions.question: What type of models does AdaBoost typically use as base estimators?
questions.explanation: AdaBoost typically uses shallow Decision Trees (often just decision stumps with max_depth=1) as weak learners.

questions.question: What is Gradient Boosting?
options: A boosting method that trains new models to predict the residual errors of the previous ensemble
questions.explanation: Gradient Boosting trains each new model to predict the residual errors (negative gradients) of the current ensemble, gradually correcting mistakes.

questions.question: What is the learning_rate (shrinkage) hyperparameter in Gradient Boosting?
options: A factor that scales each new tree's contribution, controlling overfitting
questions.explanation: Learning rate scales each tree's contribution. Lower values require more trees but often generalize better. Typical values: 0.01 to 0.3.

questions.question: What is the tradeoff between n_estimators and learning_rate in Gradient Boosting?
options: Higher learning rate requires more trees
Lower learning rate requires more trees to achieve similar performance
questions.explanation: A smaller learning rate means each tree contributes less, so you need more trees (higher n_estimators) to achieve the same ensemble strength.

questions.question: What is Stochastic Gradient Boosting?
options: Using all data for every tree
Using random subsets of data and/or features for each tree, adding randomness to Gradient Boosting
questions.explanation: Stochastic Gradient Boosting adds randomness by training each tree on a random subset of data (subsample) and/or features, improving generalization.

questions.question: What is XGBoost?
options: An optimized implementation of Gradient Boosting with regularization and efficient computing
questions.explanation: XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting) is an optimized, regularized implementation of Gradient Boosting that is fast and often wins ML competitions.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of XGBoost over standard Gradient Boosting?
options: It includes L1/L2 regularization, handles missing values automatically, and is highly optimized for speed
questions.explanation: XGBoost adds regularization (reducing overfitting), handles missing values natively, supports parallel processing, and uses various optimizations for speed.

questions.question: What is LightGBM?
options: A gradient boosting framework by Microsoft that uses histogram-based algorithms and leaf-wise tree growth for efficiency
questions.explanation: LightGBM uses histogram-based splitting and grows trees leaf-wise (not level-wise), making it much faster and more memory-efficient than traditional Gradient Boosting.

questions.question: What is CatBoost?
options: A gradient boosting library by Yandex that handles categorical features natively and reduces prediction bias
questions.explanation: CatBoost handles categorical features automatically, uses ordered boosting to reduce prediction bias, and is particularly strong on datasets with many categorical variables.

questions.question: What is Stacking (Stacked Generalization)?
options: Training models on the same data
Randomly selecting one model per prediction
Training a meta-model to combine predictions from multiple base models
questions.explanation: Stacking trains multiple diverse base models, then trains a meta-model (blender) on their predictions to make the final prediction.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the meta-learner (blender) in Stacking?
options: To learn how to best combine the predictions of base models
To select the best base model
To reduce the number of base models
questions.explanation: The meta-learner is trained on the out-of-sample predictions of base models, learning the optimal way to combine them for better performance.

questions.question: Why should you use cross-validation predictions when training the meta-learner in Stacking?
options: To prevent the meta-learner from overfitting to the training set by ensuring it sees out-of-sample predictions
To reduce the number of models
questions.explanation: Using out-of-fold/cross-validated predictions ensures the meta-learner learns from predictions on unseen data, preventing overfitting.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Bagging and Boosting?
options: Bagging trains models sequentially; Boosting trains them in parallel
Bagging trains models in parallel on bootstrap samples; Boosting trains models sequentially focusing on errors
questions.explanation: Bagging trains independent models in parallel. Boosting trains models sequentially, with each model focusing on correcting previous errors.

questions.question: Which ensemble method is most prone to overfitting: Bagging or Boosting?
questions.explanation: Boosting can overfit if too many iterations are used because it keeps trying to fit training data residuals. Bagging with randomized trees is more robust to overfitting.

questions.question: What is the purpose of warm_start in GradientBoostingClassifier?
options: To allow adding more trees to an already trained model without retraining from scratch
questions.explanation: warm_start=True allows you to incrementally add estimators to a fitted model, useful for finding the optimal number of trees via early stopping.

questions.question: What does the subsample parameter do in Gradient Boosting?
options: It sets the test set size
It specifies the fraction of training data to use for each tree (stochastic gradient boosting)
It sets the validation set size
questions.explanation: subsample < 1.0 means each tree is trained on a random subset of the training data, introducing randomness and reducing overfitting.

questions.question: In Random Forest, does increasing n_estimators always improve performance?
options: Performance improves up to a point, then plateaus; more trees only increase computation
questions.explanation: After a certain number of trees, adding more provides diminishing returns. The ensemble converges and extra trees only increase prediction time.

questions.question: What is the typical effect of increasing max_depth in Gradient Boosting?
options: It increases model complexity and risk of overfitting
questions.explanation: Deeper trees in Gradient Boosting can fit more complex patterns but also increase overfitting risk. Gradient Boosting typically uses shallower trees than Random Forest.

questions.question: What is Histogram-Based Gradient Boosting in Scikit-Learn?
options: An optimized Gradient Boosting implementation that bins continuous features into histograms for faster training
questions.explanation: HistGradientBoostingClassifier/Regressor bins continuous features into discrete bins, dramatically speeding up training while maintaining similar accuracy.

questions.question: Which ensemble method typically requires the least hyperparameter tuning?
questions.explanation: Random Forest is relatively robust and works well with default parameters. Gradient Boosting requires more careful tuning of n_estimators, learning_rate, and max_depth.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of ensemble methods compared to single models?
options: Higher computational cost and reduced interpretability
questions.explanation: Ensembles are more computationally expensive to train and harder to interpret than single models, though they usually achieve better accuracy.

questions.question: When should you prefer Gradient Boosting over Random Forest?
options: When you have time to tune hyperparameters and want potentially higher accuracy
When you need the fastest training
When you have very high-dimensional sparse data
questions.explanation: Well-tuned Gradient Boosting (e.g., XGBoost, LightGBM) often outperforms Random Forest but requires more hyperparameter tuning.

questions.question: What is the key hyperparameter to tune first in a Random Forest?
questions.explanation: n_estimators (number of trees) is usually tuned first. More trees generally improve performance until convergence, at the cost of training time.
